Event: Jaime pushes Bran out of the window to protect his affair and children
Explanation: Secures short-term secrecy for himself and Cersei but triggers a devastating war and cripples Bran.

Event: Jaime kills King Aerys to stop the wildfire plot
Explanation: Sacrifices his honor and reputation while saving hundreds of thousands from burning.

Event: Jaime attacks Ned Stark’s party in the street over Tyrion’s arrest
Explanation: Tries to avenge Tyrion by force, gains no real advantage and harms Stark household.

Event: Jaime kills Jory Cassel in front of Ned
Explanation: Escalates the feud and hurts Ned emotionally without achieving strategic benefit.

Event: Jaime spares Ned after his man stabs Ned in the leg
Explanation: Protects his own sense of honor while avoiding needless escalation by killing a wounded rival.

Event: Jaime kills his young cousin Alton to escape captivity
Explanation: Gains a chance at escape at the price of kinslaying and lifelong guilt.

Event: Jaime tries to escape Robb Stark’s camp, killing Rickard Karstark’s son
Explanation: Gets a brief escape but worsens his own position and fuels Stark hatred.

Event: Jaime warns Brienne not to fight the Bolton men planning to rape her
Explanation: Attempts to preserve her life and avoid needless slaughter while also keeping himself safer.

Event: Jaime convinces Locke not to rape Brienne, arguing she’s worth more untouched
Explanation: Uses cunning to protect Brienne without endangering himself, gaining Locke’s favor.

Event: Jaime goes back to Harrenhal to rescue Brienne from the bear pit
Explanation: Risks himself and his standing with Bolton men to save her, but succeeds and strengthens their bond.

Event: Locke cuts off Jaime’s sword hand out of spite
Explanation: Inflicts huge harm on Jaime’s value and identity while gaining status and petty satisfaction.

Event: Jaime descends into despair and refuses to eat after losing his hand
Explanation: Harms his own health and recovery with no benefit to anyone.

Event: Jaime hones his left‑handed fighting and adopts more defensive tactics
Explanation: Rebuilds competency and survivability, mildly benefiting allies who rely on him in battle.

Event: Jaime uses his golden hand as an improvised shield in combat
Explanation: Creatively exploits his handicap to survive and remain effective in a fight.

Event: Jaime is fitted with an ornamental golden hand instead of a hook
Explanation: Restores status and some utility, though it’s less practical than alternatives.

Event: Jaime kills a guard and briefly escapes while prisoner
Explanation: Gains a short-lived escape at cost of reigniting hostility and making his captors harsher.

Event: Jaime returns alone to Winterfell to fight the dead
Explanation: Risks facing enemies and judgment but increases the North’s military strength.

Event: Jaime apologizes to Bran for pushing him and admits he’s changed
Explanation: Improves his own moral clarity and preserves fragile alliance by acknowledging guilt.

Event: Bran chooses not to reveal Jaime as his attacker so Jaime can fight
Explanation: Secures a needed warrior for the battle while relinquishing personal vengeance.

Event: Jon, Sansa, and Dany accept Jaime’s service after Brienne vouches
Explanation: Gain a skilled commander with minimal cost by prioritizing survival over revenge.

Event: Brienne publicly defends Jaime at Winterfell’s hearing
Explanation: Spends her reputation to keep a proven ally in the fight, benefiting him and the cause.

Event: Jaime knights Brienne before the Long Night
Explanation: Strengthens her morale and status while enhancing his own partial redemption.

Event: Jaime and Brienne sleep together in Winterfell
Explanation: Mutual emotional and physical fulfillment before a likely death with no shown downside.

Event: Jaime abandons Brienne to return to Cersei
Explanation: Harms himself emotionally and militarily while devastating Brienne and aiding no cause.

Event: Jaime confesses to Brienne he has done monstrous things for Cersei
Explanation: Honest self-indictment clarifies his motivations and gives Brienne truthful closure.

Event: Jaime travels south to try to save Cersei from Daenerys
Explanation: Risks and ultimately loses his life for an unwinnable, doomed attempt with no payoff.

Event: Jaime dies with Cersei as the Red Keep collapses
Explanation: Fulfills his desire to die in her arms but ends his bloodline and offers no help to others.

Event: Jaime kills his first man by decapitating a Kingswood outlaw
Explanation: Eliminates a threat in line with his role, enhancing his martial standing.

Event: Jaime threatens to kill Edmure’s infant son and all Tully heirs to force surrender
Explanation: Gains Riverrun through psychological terror at the moral cost of credible atrocity.

Event: Jaime uses Edmure to retake Riverrun for the Freys and crown
Explanation: Secures a key strategic castle and reputation as effective commander by breaking the siege.

Event: Jaime gives Oathkeeper to Brienne and sends her to protect the Stark girls
Explanation: Improves his own redemption arc while materially aiding the Starks’ survival.

Event: Jaime frees Tyrion from prison before his execution
Explanation: Saves his brother’s life but indirectly causes Tywin’s death and his own exile from him.

Event: Jaime arranges with Varys to help Tyrion escape Westeros
Explanation: Loses his father’s trust and family stability while giving Tyrion a real second chance.

Event: Jaime helps Tyrion meet Tysha (rescue-sex setup) when young
Explanation: Gives Tyrion a brief taste of love that leads to profound, lasting trauma and abuse.

Event: Jaime confronts the High Sparrow and is stripped of his post
Explanation: Attempts to defend Cersei and crown but loses his Kingsguard position and status.

Event: Jaime sails to Dorne with Bronn to retrieve Myrcella
Explanation: Reasonable rescue attempt that fails, yet increases his bond with his daughter.

Event: Jaime fails to save Myrcella from poison despite retrieving her
Explanation: Suffers great personal loss while his actions have no net positive on her fate.

Event: Jaime admits to Myrcella that he is her father
Explanation: Finally gives her truth and emotional connection, briefly enriching both their lives.

Event: Jaime leads the Lannister army to sack Highgarden
Explanation: Gains gold, food, and strategic advantage for Cersei while destroying an allied house.

Event: Jaime offers Olenna a painless poison instead of Cersei’s torture
Explanation: Shows mercy that preserves his own conscience while giving her a dignified death.

Event: Jaime listens as Olenna confesses to killing Joffrey after he’s given the poison
Explanation: Gains painful knowledge he can’t use while his mercy still benefits her.

Event: Jaime confronts Walder Frey about his incompetence at the Twins
Explanation: Reasserts Lannister dominance and warns an unreliable ally, bolstering his own authority.

Event: Jaime slaps Black Walder with his golden hand for insolence
Explanation: Enforces discipline and respect among allies at minimal risk to himself.

Event: Jaime charges Daenerys while she is protected by Drogon
Explanation: Near-suicidal impulse attack that fails and almost gets him killed without gain.

Event: Jaime rallies Lannister men during Drogon’s attack on the loot train
Explanation: Leads from the front to sustain morale and cohesion under catastrophic assault.

Event: Jaime shows compassion to Dickon Tarly after his first battle
Explanation: Supports a shaken subordinate, improving loyalty and unit morale with no real cost.

Event: Jaime recognizes the existential threat of the White Walkers
Explanation: Adjusts his strategic outlook toward collective survival, influencing later choices.

Event: Jaime advises Cersei they cannot win against Daenerys’ forces
Explanation: Provides realistic counsel that could avert ruin, though she ignores it.

Event: Cersei ignores Jaime’s repeated strategic warnings about the war
Explanation: Preserves her ego and fragile power while heightening long-term risk for her side.

Event: Jaime walks out on Cersei when she refuses to help fight the dead
Explanation: Abandons toxic loyalty to join the only fight that can save the realm.

Event: Cersei threatens to have Jaime killed for leaving and then relents
Explanation: Attempts to coerce him but ultimately shows weakness, straining her last real bond.

Event: Jaime chooses to serve Cersei after she blows up the Sept with wildfire
Explanation: Continues backing an increasingly Aerys-like queen, undercutting his own values.

Event: Brienne pleads with Jaime in S8 to stay in Winterfell rather than die for Cersei
Explanation: Tries to keep him alive and on the right side, motivated by love and judgment.

Event: Jaime uses his gold hand to block a sword and save himself in Dorne
Explanation: Capitalizes on his fake hand defensively to survive an otherwise losing situation.

Event: Jaime publically supports Tyrion’s innocence at his trial
Explanation: Tries to sway outcome but fails, risking Cersei and Tywin’s anger for his brother.

Event: Jaime refuses to kill Tyrion when Cersei demands it
Explanation: Preserves his brother’s life at the cost of further alienating Cersei.

Event: Jaime threatens to murder every soul in Riverrun for Cersei
Explanation: Uses credible mass-murder threat to achieve political objectives for Cersei.

Event: Jaime saves Bronn from execution in Dorne by claiming superior responsibility
Explanation: Protects a valuable ally and fighter while taking negligible additional risk himself.

Event: Jaime mocks and then loses a verbal duel with Loras at the Purple Wedding
Explanation: Attempts to intimidate Loras, instead gets emotionally wounded with no payoff.

Event: Jaime taunts Catelyn about conflicting oaths and Ned’s infidelity
Explanation: Affirms his own worldview and pride at the cost of deepening Stark animosity.

Event: Jaime confides his true reason for killing Aerys to Brienne
Explanation: Shares the hidden truth, improving her understanding and partially restoring his honor.

Event: Jaime insults Brienne early in their journey to provoke her
Explanation: Amuses his ego but worsens their relationship with no strategic benefit.

Event: Jaime gradually comes to care for Brienne and repeatedly protects her
Explanation: Invests in a loyal, capable ally while advancing his own redemption.

Event: Jaime tells Bronn he wants to die in the arms of the woman he loves
Explanation: Reveals his fatalistic wish, which offers emotional clarity but leads to no gain.

Event: Jaime openly derides Walder Frey as a coward and liability
Explanation: Signals Lannister dominance and warns an unreliable ally, bolstering his own image.

Event: Jaime keeps silent for years about Aerys’ wildfire plot and his motives
Explanation: Clings to pride while allowing his reputation to rot despite his heroic act.

Event: Jaime tells Bronn in S8 he would kill Tyrion if seen again, but later doesn’t
Explanation: Recognizes his better nature and refuses to act on vengeful rhetoric.

Event: Jaime and Tyrion reconcile in S7 and exchange a final hug in S8
Explanation: Mutually restore brotherly bond and gain emotional closure before war.

Event: Jaime returns to Cersei in S8 claiming he never cared for innocents
Explanation: Denies his most heroic act and sacrifices growth to re-embrace a destructive identity.
